1920s.label: 1920s
1920s.tagline: The Roaring Twenties & The Flapper Revolution
1920s.context: The 1920s brought seismic cultural shifts. Women won the right to vote in 1920 (19th Amendment), jazz music filled speakeasies, and a new feminine archetype — the flapper — challenged Victorian propriety with bobbed hair, shorter skirts, and public independence. Yet these freedoms were largely for white, middle-class women. Working-class and Black women had long labored outside the home out of economic necessity, with little social reward. Marriage was still the primary social contract for most women; divorce was shameful. Men were expected to be breadwinners, stoic, and authoritative. The KKK resurged, and racial gender norms were enforced through violence.

women: Marry young and prioritize homemaking
Be sexually modest and virtuous (unless you were a 'flapper')
Defer to husbands in financial and legal matters
Avoid ambition beyond the domestic sphere
Embody the 'new woman' ideal: slim, modern, but still marriageable
men: Be the sole financial provider for the family
Display stoic emotional control — no crying, no vulnerability
Lead the household as the unquestioned authority
Pursue career ambition as a moral duty
Maintain masculine appearance; effeminacy was shamed

dominant_expectations: Women's primary identity was wife and mother
Men who couldn't support a family were seen as failures
Public displays of non-heterosexuality were criminal
Women's legal rights were largely tied to their husbands
Race strictly determined what gender freedoms were available
key_events: 19th Amendment (1920): Women gain the right to vote
Prohibition era gives women new public spaces (speakeasies)
Flapper culture challenges Victorian femininity
Margaret Sanger opens first birth control clinic (1916, movement grows in 20s)
First woman elected to U.S. Senate: Rebecca Felton (1922)
cultural_touchstones: The Great Gatsby (Fitzgerald, 1925)
Josephine Baker — Black feminine icon abroad
Coco Chanel redefines women's fashion
Buster Keaton and Charlie Chaplin — stoic masculine ideals

1930s.label: 1930s
1930s.tagline: The Great Depression & The Retreat to Tradition
1930s.context: The Great Depression devastated the economy and paradoxically hardened gender roles. As men lost jobs, their masculine identity was threatened. Many Americans blamed women workers for 'stealing' jobs from men. Laws were passed in many states barring married women from government jobs. At the same time, women shouldered enormous domestic burdens — stretching scarce resources, managing households under extreme stress — largely invisible and uncompensated. Roosevelt's New Deal programs often excluded women and minorities. The era's cultural message was clear: women belonged in the home, and men who couldn't provide were failures.

women: Leave paid work upon marriage (or be seen as selfish)
Stretch household resources through domestic skill and sacrifice
Subordinate personal ambitions to family survival
Embody cheerful resilience regardless of hardship
Avoid competing with men for scarce employment
men: Bear personal shame for unemployment, even in systemic collapse
Provide financially at all costs — failure to do so was emasculating
Suppress emotional distress about economic hardship
Maintain authority even without economic power
Be the moral and practical head of household

dominant_expectations: Married women should not work if their husband is employed
Men's worth was inseparable from their earning capacity
Divorce remained deeply stigmatized
Homosexuality was criminalized and pathologized
Breadwinner masculinity was a cultural obsession
key_events: Stock market crash (1929) triggers mass unemployment
Economy Act (1932) bars married women from federal employment
Frances Perkins becomes first female Cabinet secretary (1933)
New Deal programs largely exclude domestic and agricultural workers (disproportionately women and minorities)
Dust Bowl forces massive family migration and stress
cultural_touchstones: The Grapes of Wrath (Steinbeck, 1939)
Dorothea Lange's 'Migrant Mother' photograph
Shirley Temple — idealized innocent femininity
Clark Gable — rugged, dominant masculine ideal

1940s.label: 1940s
1940s.tagline: World War II & Rosie the Riveter
1940s.context: World War II forced a dramatic — if temporary — renegotiation of gender roles. With millions of men deployed, women flooded into manufacturing, aviation, and government roles previously barred to them. Rosie the Riveter became a cultural icon, glorifying women's industrial labor. Yet this was framed as a wartime exception, not a permanent change. Black women and women of color did this work with far less recognition. When men returned from the front, immense social pressure pushed women back into the home. Many women who had found economic independence and professional satisfaction were quietly forced out of the workforce. Veterans returning with PTSD received no recognition of their trauma — stoicism was mandatory.

women: Sacrifice for the nation by working while men are away
Be ready to return to the home when men come back
Support the war effort as wives, mothers, and workers
Maintain femininity even while doing 'masculine' work
Greet returning veterans with uncomplicated domestic joy
men: Serve militarily as the pinnacle of masculine duty
Return home as the unquestioned head of household
Never show fear, trauma, or emotional damage from war
Resume provider role immediately and without complaint
Embody strength, heroism, and patriotic sacrifice

dominant_expectations: Women's wartime work was temporary and patriotic, not emancipatory
Male veterans deserved economic priority upon return
War trauma in men was suppressed — stoicism was mandatory
Homosexuality in the military was grounds for dishonorable discharge
Race-segregated units meant Black men served but were still denied rights
key_events: U.S. enters WWII (1941): women enter workforce en masse
6 million women join the labor force during war years
Women Airforce Service Pilots (WASPs) fly military aircraft
G.I. Bill (1944) overwhelmingly benefits returning white male veterans
Baby boom begins as veterans return and women are pushed home
cultural_touchstones: Rosie the Riveter (J. Howard Miller poster, 1943)
Since You Went Away (1944 film)
Rita Hayworth — the WWII pin-up ideal
Humphrey Bogart — the tough, emotionally closed masculine hero

1950s.label: 1950s
1950s.tagline: The Postwar Domestic Ideal & The Feminine Mystique
1950s.context: The 1950s represent the apex of rigid, binary gender norms in 20th-century America. The postwar economic boom funded a suburban ideal: a breadwinner husband, a housewife mother, 2.5 children, a house with a white picket fence. This domestic script was aggressively marketed through television, advertising, and cultural pressure. Betty Friedan would later call the suffocating experience of educated women trapped at home 'the problem with no name.' Women who stepped outside these norms faced social ostracism, psychiatric institutionalization, or both. Meanwhile, men suppressed all emotion and identity outside provider/protector roles. The Lavender Scare led to mass firings of gay federal employees. This era's conformity hid enormous anxiety and dissatisfaction.

women: Be a full-time housewife and mother — this is your highest calling
Support your husband's career unconditionally
Never outshine your husband intellectually or professionally
Maintain a perfectly kept home and have dinner ready when he arrives
Suppress personal ambitions, desires, and dissatisfaction — smile
men: Be the sole provider; a wife working implies your failure
Be emotionally unavailable — stoicism is masculinity
Lead the family with unquestioned authority
Conform to the organization man: reliable, corporate, conservative
Never show any femininity, sensitivity, or homosexual tendencies

dominant_expectations: Marriage was mandatory for respectability; spinsterhood was shameful
Homosexuality was a psychiatric disorder and criminal offense
Divorce meant social ruin, especially for women
Women had almost no reproductive autonomy (no birth control pill until 1960)
The 'ideal' family was white, suburban, and middle-class
key_events: Suburban explosion: Levittown and cookie-cutter domestic life
Lavender Scare: thousands of gay federal employees fired (1950s)
Housewife as cultural ideal saturates television and advertising
Rosa Parks refuses to give up her bus seat (1955)
Betty Friedan begins research that leads to The Feminine Mystique
cultural_touchstones: Leave It to Beaver (1957–1963 TV show)
The Man in the Gray Flannel Suit (Wilson, 1955)
Doris Day — wholesome, cheerful domestic femininity
James Dean — rebel masculinity as counterpoint to conformity

1960s.label: 1960s
1960s.context: The 1960s cracked the postwar domestic consensus open. The birth control pill (1960) gave women unprecedented reproductive autonomy. Betty Friedan's 'The Feminine Mystique' (1963) named the suffocating domesticity millions felt. The National Organization for Women was founded in 1966. Civil rights movements forced conversations about intersecting oppressions of race and gender. The sexual revolution loosened sexual norms — but often more for men than women, as women who embraced sexual freedom were still labeled as promiscuous. Counterculture movements questioned authority and conformity. The Vietnam War forced reckoning with mandatory masculinity. Yet progress was uneven: abortion remained illegal, and women still faced legal discrimination in credit, housing, and employment.

women: The housewife ideal is challenged but still dominant for many
Education and career ambition becomes more acceptable
Sexual freedom increasing, but double standard remains fierce
Expected to be pretty, thin, and fashionable (Twiggy era)
Black women expected to serve both racial and gender liberation movements
men: Traditional breadwinner role is questioned by some, reinforced by others
Vietnam War: military service as masculine duty vs. conscientious objection
Counterculture men challenge conformity but often maintain gender hierarchies
Emotional expression slightly more accepted in some subcultures
Sexual dominance and conquest remain normalized masculine behaviors

dominant_expectations: Women's liberation is a growing movement but still radical to most Americans
Birth control gives women agency but is still stigmatized for unmarried women
Homosexuality remains illegal in most states and classified as mental illness
The sexual revolution primarily benefited heterosexual men
Race and gender liberation are inseparable but often treated as separate causes
key_events: FDA approves the birth control pill for married women (1960)
Betty Friedan publishes The Feminine Mystique (1963)
Equal Pay Act (1963) — largely unenforced but symbolically important
Civil Rights Act (1964) — includes sex discrimination provision
NOW (National Organization for Women) founded (1966)
Stonewall Riots launch LGBTQ+ liberation movement (1969)
cultural_touchstones: The Feminine Mystique (Friedan, 1963)
Twiggy — ultra-thin model redefines femininity
James Brown — Black masculine pride
Easy Rider (1969) — masculine freedom and the open road

1970s.label: 1970s
1970s.tagline: Women's Liberation & The Fight for Equality
1970s.context: The 1970s were the height of second-wave feminism. The women's liberation movement was in full force: consciousness-raising groups, protests, and landmark legal victories reshaped American life. Roe v. Wade (1973) secured abortion rights. Title IX (1972) opened sports and education. The Equal Rights Amendment passed Congress in 1972 (though it failed ratification). Ms. Magazine launched. Women entered law, medicine, and business in growing numbers. Men's liberation movements and the early gay rights movement questioned masculine scripts. The gay liberation movement exploded post-Stonewall. Yet backlash was fierce: Phyllis Schlafly organized against ERA, and 'traditional family values' became a political weapon. For women of color, feminist progress often still centered white women's experiences.

women: Career ambitions are increasingly legitimate and celebrated
Reproductive autonomy is a fought-for right
Sisterhood and solidarity with other women is a value
Appearance pressure remains but is questioned by feminists
Sexuality can be expressed more openly — though lesbian identity still risky
men: Traditional masculinity is explicitly questioned by men's liberation writers
Emotional sensitivity becomes slightly more acceptable in some circles
Gay men have a visible liberation movement for the first time
Provider identity is challenged as women enter the workforce
Macho culture and backlash masculinity also intensify in response

dominant_expectations: Women can and should pursue equality, but it's still a radical claim to many
Gay people are increasingly visible but still face massive discrimination
Domestic violence and marital rape are barely recognized as crimes
Men who share domestic labor are seen as unusual or weak
Race continues to determine access to feminist progress
key_events: Roe v. Wade legalizes abortion nationwide (1973)
Title IX prohibits sex discrimination in education (1972)
ERA passes Congress, begins unsuccessful ratification battle (1972)
Ms. Magazine founded by Gloria Steinem (1972)
First domestic violence shelters open in the U.S.
Harvey Milk elected as first openly gay official in California (1977)
cultural_touchstones: Ms. Magazine and Gloria Steinem
Billie Jean King defeats Bobby Riggs in 'Battle of the Sexes' (1973)
Free to Be You and Me (Marlo Thomas, 1972)
Saturday Night Fever (1977) — disco masculinity

1980s.label: 1980s
1980s.tagline: Power Suits, AIDS Crisis & The Backlash Years
1980s.context: The 1980s were a decade of contradiction. Women entered professional life in record numbers, donning power suits and 'having it all' — careers, families, and perfect appearances simultaneously. Yet Susan Faludi would document the 1980s as a decade of backlash: cultural messaging relentlessly told women that feminism had made them unhappy, unmarriageable, and infertile. The AIDS crisis devastated the gay community while Reagan's government ignored it for years — a genocidal indifference rooted in homophobia. Religious conservatism surged with the Moral Majority. 'Family values' became a political code word targeting women's autonomy and LGBTQ+ people. Beauty standards intensified: thinness, youth, and styled femininity were demanded of professional women simultaneously with professional competence.

women: 'Superwoman' ideal: career + perfect wife + perfect mother simultaneously
Professional success acceptable but you must also be beautiful and domestic
Power suits signal authority, but you cannot actually be 'too aggressive'
Appearance standards are intense: thin, styled, made-up, young
Reproductive choices face renewed political attack
men: Financial success and dominance are the ultimate masculine values
Emotional vulnerability = weakness; hyper-masculinity resurges (Rambo era)
Gay men face AIDS-era stigma and renewed social violence
The 'sensitive man' of the 70s is mocked; machismo returns
Men who participate in childcare are 'Mr. Moms' — a joke

dominant_expectations: Women can work, but having it 'all' comes at enormous personal cost
Homosexuality is associated with AIDS, shame, and death in public discourse
Conservative family values: heterosexual marriage, traditional roles
The beauty industry explodes: women owe the world thinness and youth
Crack epidemic and Reagan-era policies devastate communities of color, especially women
key_events: Reagan elected; Moral Majority rises; backlash against feminism begins (1980)
AIDS crisis erupts; Reagan ignores it until 1987
Sandra Day O'Connor becomes first female Supreme Court Justice (1981)
Geraldine Ferraro nominated as first female VP candidate (1984)
Susan Faludi begins research for Backlash (published 1991)
ACT UP founded in response to AIDS crisis (1987)
cultural_touchstones: Working Girl (1988 film)
Rambo — hypermasculine backlash figure
Madonna — female sexuality as power and provocation
Dynasty and Dallas — wealth, power, and gender performance

1990s.label: 1990s
1990s.tagline: Third-Wave Feminism, Girl Power & The Culture Wars
1990s.context: The 1990s brought third-wave feminism, which centered personal choice, intersectionality, and reclaiming sexuality as power. 'Girl Power' (popularized by the Spice Girls) and riot grrrl punk rock gave young women assertive identities. The Anita Hill hearings (1991) put workplace sexual harassment on the national stage. The Violence Against Women Act (1994) was landmark legislation. LGBTQ+ visibility grew, with Ellen DeGeneres coming out in 1997. Yet 'don't ask, don't tell' kept military LGBTQ+ identity hidden. Pop culture celebrated hyper-thin beauty standards (heroin chic) while also selling 'empowerment.' Men were still largely expected to embody stoic success, though the 'sensitive man' had small cultural space. Intersectionality became a key feminist concept (coined by Kimberlé Crenshaw in 1989).

women: Career ambition is fully normalized for most women
Girl power: assertiveness and ambition are celebrated (within limits)
Sexuality can be a source of power — but you can still be slut-shamed
Extreme thinness and youth remain dominant beauty demands
Motherhood and career still pitched as an impossible trade-off
men: Sensitive New Age Guy (SNAG) exists but is still somewhat mocked
Emotional expression is slightly more acceptable in close relationships
Gay identity is increasingly visible, though discrimination remains intense
Sports and financial success remain the main masculine status markers
Men who take paternity leave or do primary childcare are still unusual

dominant_expectations: Third-wave feminism says women can do anything — but must also look perfect
Gay marriage is unthinkable to most Americans; DADT enforces military closet
Welfare queen stereotype is weaponized against poor women of color
Violence against women is finally named as a crisis (VAWA, 1994)
Intersectionality as concept challenges white-centric feminism
key_events: Anita Hill testifies at Clarence Thomas hearings (1991)
Riot grrrl movement and Lilith Fair challenge music industry sexism
Family and Medical Leave Act (1993) — unpaid, limited
Violence Against Women Act (1994)
Ellen DeGeneres publicly comes out (1997)
Monica Lewinsky scandal exposes sexual double standards in power
cultural_touchstones: Spice Girls: 'Wannabe' and girl power marketing
Thelma & Louise (1991) — female rage and freedom
Reality Bites (1994) — Generation X disillusionment
Friends — heteronormative aspirational young adulthood

2000s.label: 2000s
2000s.tagline: Post-9/11 Culture & Early Social Media Gender Norms
2000s.context: The 2000s opened with a contested election and were reshaped by 9/11, which produced a surge in hypermasculine militarism and a retreat to 'traditional' gender roles in patriotic imagery. Reality TV exploded, packaging gender performance as entertainment. The early internet and social media began reshaping beauty standards and identity politics. Sarah Palin ran for Vice President, making both feminism's advances and its limits visible. Same-sex marriage became a major cultural battleground, with Massachusetts legalizing it in 2004. Celebrity culture celebrated 'hot mess' femininity (Paris Hilton) alongside 'strong women.' Men's roles were little examined. The phrase 'metrosexual' briefly acknowledged that straight men could care about appearance, but broader masculine emotional expression remained constrained.

women: Career is expected, but 'opting out' narrative pressures educated mothers to stay home
Hypersexualized appearance norms intensify via celebrity and reality TV culture
Political ambition is increasingly legible but still met with sexist commentary
Online spaces create new arenas for gender policing and harassment
Girlboss feminism: individual success as the measure of liberation
men: Post-9/11 hypermasculinity: warrior/protector role surges in cultural value
Metrosexual masculinity gives slight permission for grooming attention
Emotional vulnerability remains largely private or suppressed
Video games and the internet create new masculine identity spaces
Financial success and sexual dominance remain core status markers

dominant_expectations: Gay marriage is debated nationally; most states ban it via constitutional amendments
Don't Ask Don't Tell keeps LGBTQ+ military members closeted
Post-9/11 rhetoric reinforces domestic roles for women in contrast to male warriors
Social media begins amplifying appearance-based judgment of women
Race and gender intersect in Hurricane Katrina response failures
key_events: Massachusetts legalizes same-sex marriage (2004)
Sarah Palin nominated for VP (2008) — first Republican woman on national ticket
Facebook (2004) and YouTube (2005) launch, reshaping gender performance online
Hurricane Katrina exposes racial-gendered poverty (2005)
Hillary Clinton runs for president (2008), hits 'glass ceiling'
Obama elected first Black president (2008)
cultural_touchstones: Legally Blonde (2001) — feminism as individual girl-boss achievement
Paris Hilton and celebrity-driven hypersexual femininity
Grey's Anatomy — women in medicine, complex gender dynamics
Brokeback Mountain (2005) — gay masculinity breaks into mainstream cinema

2010s.label: 2010s
2010s.context: The 2010s were a decade of accelerating gender and sexuality progress alongside fierce backlash. Same-sex marriage was legalized nationwide in 2015 (Obergefell). Transgender visibility grew dramatically, from Laverne Cox on magazine covers to legal battles over bathroom access. #MeToo (2017) exploded into public consciousness, naming sexual harassment and assault as systemic. Social media became a primary arena for gender politics — calling out, cancel culture, and feminist discourse. Genderqueer and nonbinary identities gained cultural visibility. Yet the 2016 election — with its pussy-grabbing audio and defeat of the first female major-party presidential candidate — revealed the depth of gender-based backlash. Movements like Black Lives Matter centered Black women's leadership and intersectionality.

women: Lean In feminism: individual ambition as liberation (but critiqued for ignoring systemic barriers)
#MeToo names sexual violence in workplaces, entertainment, and everyday life
Appearance pressure intensifies via Instagram, filtered selfie culture
Gender-nonconforming and trans women gain visibility and face intense backlash
Intersectionality increasingly expected in mainstream feminist discourse
men: Toxic masculinity becomes a named concept in cultural conversation
Men's rights backlash and 'men going their own way' movements emerge
Gay and bi men are increasingly visible and somewhat integrated
Millennial and Gen Z men show slightly more comfort with emotional expression
The 'man box' — rigid masculine scripts — is being critically examined

dominant_expectations: Same-sex relationships have full legal recognition — but social acceptance is uneven
Trans people face bathroom bills, military bans, and political targeting
Reproductive rights face renewed state-level attack
Nonbinary gender identity is culturally visible but legally unrecognized in most places
Performative vs. structural feminism is a live debate
key_events: Obergefell v. Hodges: same-sex marriage legalized nationwide (2015)
Caitlyn Jenner publicly transitions; trans visibility surges (2015)
#MeToo movement explodes after Harvey Weinstein reporting (2017)
Women's March (2017) — largest single-day protest in U.S. history
Record number of women run for and win congressional seats (2018)
Obama removes Don't Ask Don't Tell (2010)
cultural_touchstones: Orange Is the New Black (2013) — gender, race, sexuality in carceral system
Beyoncé's LEMONADE (2016) — Black feminism and womanhood
Pose (2018) — trans women of color and ballroom culture
Fleabag (2016) — raw, unsentimental female interiority

2020s.label: 2020s
2020s.tagline: Pandemic Reckoning, Gender Fluidity & The Ongoing Struggle
2020s.context: The early 2020s brought simultaneous progress and rollback. The COVID-19 pandemic exposed deep gender inequality: women bore disproportionate childcare and caregiving burdens, and millions left the workforce. The Supreme Court overturned Roe v. Wade in 2022 (Dobbs), eliminating federal abortion rights after nearly 50 years. Yet nonbinary and gender-fluid identities gained new mainstream acceptance, especially among younger generations. Gen Z is the most gender-expansive generation in recorded history. Trans rights became the frontline of culture war politics. Record numbers of women hold political office. The word 'they' as a singular pronoun gained mainstream legitimacy. The ongoing reckoning includes whether structural change can keep pace with cultural evolution — and whether liberation for some can translate to liberation for all.

women: COVID-19 pandemic forces millions of women out of the workforce for caregiving
Dobbs (2022) eliminates federal abortion rights — reproductive autonomy in crisis
Record political representation, but systemic barriers remain
Body positivity movement challenges but hasn't replaced narrow beauty standards
Mental health and boundaries are more normalized as topics
men: Men's mental health is a growing public conversation
Younger men increasingly reject rigid masculine scripts
Anti-feminist manosphere (Andrew Tate, incel culture) offers reactionary masculinity
Trans men and nonbinary AMAB people challenge what 'man' means
Emotional vulnerability in men is more accepted — but not universally

dominant_expectations: Gender-neutral and nonbinary identities are mainstream for Gen Z but contested politically
Roe v. Wade overturned: reproductive rights are now a state-by-state patchwork
Trans rights are under sustained legislative attack in multiple states
Pandemic has set women's career and economic progress back years
Pay gap persists; care work is still undervalued and disproportionately done by women
key_events: COVID-19 pandemic (2020): women leave workforce at twice the rate of men
Record number of women serve in 117th Congress
Dobbs v. Jackson overturns Roe v. Wade (2022)
Over 500 anti-trans bills introduced across U.S. states (2023)
Kamala Harris elected first female VP (2020)
Sam Smith and others popularize nonbinary public identity
cultural_touchstones: Euphoria (2019–2022) — gender fluidity among Gen Z
RuPaul's Drag Race — drag as mainstream entertainment
Barbie (2023 film) — feminist critique through popular culture
The Handmaid's Tale as recurring protest imagery post-Dobbs
